sourceMovie.title: Snake Eyes
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Snake Eyes (1998) stuck because it commits to its crime premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Brian De Palma's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The crime and mystery blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Lucky Number Slevin
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Snake Eyes clicked for you, Lucky Number Slevin (2006) is a strong next stop: both trade in crime and mystery, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Snake Eyes?
faq.answer: Try Lucky Number Slevin, Blood Work, Bordertown—each shares DNA with Snake Eyes while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Snake Eyes?
faq.answer: Snake Eyes (1998) is best described as Crime, Mystery, Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Snake Eyes worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 6.2/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this crime lane.
